paragraphs.text: Motion on the DFA objectives on underage users, the role of media and digital literacy and the division of labour between the European Union and its Member States
Signed by: Flemish People’s Representative Freya Perdaens (N-VA)
Problem Definition

evidence_quotes.quote: unwanted methods of digital service providers

paragraphs.text: The European Commission is considering a proposal for a regulation, the Digital Fairness Act (DFA), which aims to develop regulation to address unwanted methods of digital service providers in order to better protect consumers of digital service providers, in particular minors.

evidence_quotes.quote: The piest of a harsh ban for young people on social media up to the age of 16 appears to be gaining popularity.
minimum age limit to create a social media account.

paragraphs.text: Protecting minors is a notable intention. But we must be aware that this intention does not lead to particularly strict regulation, which deprives minors above all of their rights, rather than gives them opportunities. The piest of a harsh ban for young people on social media up to the age of 16 appears to be gaining popularity. Several other countries, including Australia or European Member States such as France, the Netherlands and Denmark, are actively developing regulations that in one way or another raise the minimum age limit to create a social media account. This is a concern for the petitioner.

evidence_quotes.quote: raising the age limit and requiring age verification systems, such as the Jutland Declaration1 for social media platforms as a solution
the way in which age verification is to be carried out
the impact on the privacy issues that may be associated with such a system
the enforceability of data processing and how social media will be organised for those who have access to it

paragraphs.text: Indeed, the question is whether raising the age limit and requiring age verification systems, such as the Jutland Declaration1 for social media platforms as a solution, will effectively contribute to a more secure digital environment for young people. At the very least, such an approach leads to many other questions, such as the way in which age verification is to be carried out, the impact on the privacy issues that may be associated with such a system, the enforceability of data processing and how social media will be organised for those who have access to it.

The petitioner is concerned that the answers contained in a DFA are undermining or even turning back a number of other European initiatives in the context of the online protection of minors. This includes the steps taken to respect and reaffirm young people in the digital age.2 A DFA also threatens to create the effect of a 'prohibited fruit' through excessively stringent measures and to encourage the use of methods to circumvent measures, such as VPNs or browsers such as TOR. 3

The combination of fear due to strong repressive legislation and unsuitable content due to the circumvention of repressive measures may lead to a general situation where young people are just worse off. Not only would they have access to the harmful content for which a DFA tries to shield them, they would hide it, without a framework to prevent them from doing so. In doing so, a DFA would achieve the opposite effect of what is the initial intention of the Regulation.

The effectiveness of a DFA depends on the scope of digital service providers it uses and the degree of proportionality it allows in its regulations. In other words: the more nuanced, the more effective.
Objectives of the policy to be pursued with regard to minor users
Basic principles
Rather than a repressive attitude towards the minor user in order to safeguard the safety and health of

BIK + Strategy and European Declaration on Digital Rights and Principles for the Digital Decade (2023/C 23/01, Official Journal C 23, 23 January 2023). European Union. See https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX%3A32023C0123%2801%29 3 KU Leuven Institute for Child and Youth. 2024. The sentence and invocation of the ban on social media for Australian adolescents. KU Leuven. Available at: https://www.kuleuven.be/child-youth/nl/blog/posts/social_media_offer

that user, the petitioner calls for the right to freedom of expression and privacy to be guaranteed as much as possible, while at the same time providing sufficient protection to our young people online. Therefore, the petitioner calls for an effective, privacy-sensitive and proportionate age check: an approach that provides protection where needed, while respecting fundamental rights and freedom. Depending on the content and risk of a platform, different methods of age verification are desirable, as defined in the DSA.
For concrete ages, we also stick to the framework set out in the DSA:

evidence_quotes.quote: the minimum age for minors to independently create an account at least 13 years

paragraphs.text: Leave the choice to Member States to impose the minimum age for minors to independently create an account at least 13 years;

evidence_quotes.quote: Implicit parental consent is required for the creation of an account for minors under the age of 13.

paragraphs.text: Implicit parental consent is required for the creation of an account for minors under the age of 13.

evidence_quotes.quote: each age check used:

paragraphs.text: In doing so, we would like to emphasise the DSA guidelines, which state that each age check used:
be accurate, reliable and robust;
minimise the interference with users' rights (such as privacy and data protection);

evidence_quotes.quote: should not discriminate against certain user groups.

paragraphs.text: and should not discriminate against certain user groups.
In addition, Article 35 of the DSA applies to the VLOPs (Very Large Online Platforms):

'Providers of very large online platforms and of very large online search engines shall take reasonable, proportionate and effective mitigation measures, tailored to the specific systemic risks identified pursuant to Article 34, with particular regard to the impact of those measures on fundamental rights. Such measures shall include, where applicable:

evidence_quotes.quote: parental control tools

paragraphs.text: (j) taking targeted measures to protect the rights of the child, including age control and parental control tools, or tools to help minors report abuse or receive support, as appropriate:"
On the basis of this article, the European Commission launched an investigation earlier this year into four major pornosites: PornHub, Stripchat, Xnxx and XVideos.

evidence_quotes.quote: Age verification is the most intrusive form of control

paragraphs.text: As already stated, the DSA distinguishes between three ways of carrying out age checks online: self-declaration, Age Estimate or Age Verification. Age verification is the most intrusive form of control, only proportionate/appropriate when accessing 18 + services such as pornography, gambling or alcohol sales. The three methods are increasingly ways to obtain certainty about the age of the user. According to the philosophy behind the DSA, the intention is to make an appropriate and proportionate choice between these three age-checking methods depending on the content offered by a digital service provider.

section_path: Self-declaration, Age Estimate or Age Verification?

evidence_quotes.quote: the most intrusive form of age verification

paragraphs.text: The Jutland Declaration, which nevertheless presents itself as an informal and preliminary European Council position on a DFA, assumes that only age verification, the most intrusive form of age verification, is sufficiently appropriate and proportionate to ensure sufficient online protection for minors. This seems to us to be at least inconsistent with the DSA, which states that age-checking should be chosen depending on the risk and nature of the content offered by the digital service provider. In addition, we fear interpretation and application problems in the application of new

section_path: Self-declaration, Age Estimate or Age Verification?

paragraphs.text: standards that would be defined in a DFA in combination with existing ones as required by the DSA.

section_path: Self-declaration, Age Estimate or Age Verification?

evidence_quotes.quote: considering age estimation as the standard mechanism
age verification only necessary in a clear 18+ context (pornography, addictive agents, gambling, etc.).

paragraphs.text: Therefore, the petitioner fully advocates the DSA as the only starting point for determining the most effective age control mechanism, considering age estimation as the standard mechanism and considering age verification only necessary in a clear 18+ context (pornography, addictive agents, gambling, etc.).

section_path: Self-declaration, Age Estimate or Age Verification?

evidence_quotes.quote: robust age verification model to protect minors.

paragraphs.text: As stated above, discussions on online safety for minors tend to focus on social media platforms. That safety is divided into two aspects: the architecture of the digital service provider, and the content that can be found on it. In Flanders too, this is the case in the public debate. For example, there is an open letter from Kids unplugged, an association of worried parents, calling for a robust age verification model to protect minors. 4

section_path: Definition of ‘social medium’ and ‘digital services relevant for minors’

evidence_quotes.quote: not to be immediately included in the call for a strict age verification system,

paragraphs.text: On the other hand, the opinion of the Flemish Youth Council, the official advisory council of the Flemish Government for children and young people, calls for not to be immediately included in the call for a strict age verification system, including positive effects of social media presence and fundamental youth and children’s rights.5 The Media Council, part of the Flemish Government’s Strategic Advisory Council for Culture, Youth, Sport and Media, also calls for a purely repressive approach to the safety of minors on social media and, at least, for more research before taking decisions. 6

section_path: Definition of ‘social medium’ and ‘digital services relevant for minors’

paragraphs.text: It should be noted that, according to the petitioner, the Media Council rightly indicates that limiting the discussion on online security to social media platforms requires too narrow a view, and above all leaves a wide range of digital services out of scope.

section_path: Definition of ‘social medium’ and ‘digital services relevant for minors’

evidence_quotes.quote: games with online multiplying function and extensive means of interaction (live audio, video, text messages, etc.).

paragraphs.text: Indeed, there are sufficient examples of digital services that have features of what is commonly considered as a ‘social medium’, but it is not. Think of the comment section on newspaper websites or web shops, internet forums with extensive possibilities to attach media items to a particular post or games with online multiplying function and extensive means of interaction (live audio, video, text messages, etc.). Messaging apps such as WhatsApp, Signal, Telegram and Trema are also increasingly expanding the possibilities for communication groups.

section_path: Definition of ‘social medium’ and ‘digital services relevant for minors’

evidence_quotes.quote: an age verification system

paragraphs.text: In other words: the line of what is a social medium is difficult to draw. In extreme terms, this could result in any digital service being subject to an age verification system, which, in our view, goes far beyond the purpose of a DFA.

section_path: Definition of ‘social medium’ and ‘digital services relevant for minors’

evidence_quotes.quote: a delineation of digital services that would not be suitable for minors.

paragraphs.text: Therefore, the petitioner opposes a delineation of digital services that would not be suitable for minors. Firstly, because the assessment of adequacy for minors must be made on the basis of the content, and this, according to the petitioner, is a competence of the Member States. Moreover, such an exercise seems impossible because each digital service has characteristics of a social medium to a greater or lesser extent, making strict delimitation incompatible with the principle of proportionality set out in the DSA.

section_path: Definition of ‘social medium’ and ‘digital services relevant for minors’

paragraphs.text: 4 https://www.kidsunplugged.be/nl/nieuws 5 https://vlaamsejeugdraad.be/nl/adviezen/worden-we-geghost-scroll-niet-voorbij-onze-stem 6 https://www.vlaanderen.be/sarc/sites/sarc/files/2025- 09/2025 09 25 opini op ow initi children en y en sociale media.pdf

Existing European legislation already provides for different enforcement possibilities for different types of digital service providers, both at European level and through the implementation of regulations and directives at Member State level.

To date, the logic is that Europe can impose sanctions on digital service providers who do not comply with European rules. Member States also have sanctioning possibilities, but their competent regulators can also step up their intervention to ban a particular digital service provider.

evidence_quotes.quote: clear terms of use on algorithm transparency
clear rules around adequate and pro-consumer privacy and use settings
an opt-in philosophy rather than an opt-out philosophy

paragraphs.text: This logic is supported by the petitioner. In this context, the Commission sees a key role for the European level with a DFA in establishing a clear framework and standards for digital service providers to adhere to fair market practices. This goes from clear terms of use on algorithm transparency to clear rules around adequate and pro-consumer privacy and use settings, following an opt-in philosophy rather than an opt-out philosophy. Penalties for non-compliance with these standards can be e.g. fines.

At Member State level, European guidance can contribute to the consideration of further enforcement and sanctioning measures that Member States wish to apply. With regard specifically to the debate on the age control method used, this choice should also be left to Member States, rather than by means of a regulation at European level. This reflects much better the situation on the ground, where some Member States are already further in the digitalisation agenda, insights on media and media literacy of the population.

Moreover, this dichotomy places much better responsibility for a secure online environment on the digital service providers themselves, rather than on public authorities, by forcing digital service providers to create an online environment that is considered acceptable by both the European Union and the individual Member States. In doing so, the petitioner relies on the reasoning that a top-down imposition of an age verification mechanism in the context of the protection of minors who, while depriving minors of harmful content, does not remove the harmful content from the digital environment. However, the latter should be the DFA's input.

evidence_quotes.quote: It is not, therefore, a matter for service distributors themselves to moderate content, let alone to use untrustworthy AI tools.

paragraphs.text: The petitioner is not in favour of content control and puts freedom of expression first. In the case of legally defined criminal judgments (e.g. negationism), it is the court that has to rule on the content on which a complaint has been lodged. It is not, therefore, a matter for service distributors themselves to moderate content, let alone to use untrustworthy AI tools. She is also aware that a different choice has been made in DSA.

In the context of both the DSA and AVMD obligations to provide appropriate measures for minors not to transmit harmful content to them, as well as self-regulatory initiatives, many social media platforms already provide for content control mechanisms. Until recently, it was mainly staff of the digital service providers who had to check content and declare their suitability through tagging.

evidence_quotes.quote: This is often due to the difficulty AI faces in recognising certain circumvention tactics of users: think of the use of loaded, discriminatory words from smaller languages that AI does not recognise as quickly.

paragraphs.text: The rise of AI has largely automated these control mechanisms. Not only has this led to many dismissals, it also reduced the effectiveness of control mechanisms. This is often due to the difficulty AI faces in recognising certain circumvention tactics of users: think of the use of loaded, discriminatory words from smaller languages that AI does not recognise as quickly.7 And if AI has
7 CFR Parliamentary Question No 2868 by Freya Perdaens to Flemish Minister for Media Cielje Van Achter dated 22/05/2025 (https://www.vlaamsparlement.be/nl/parlementair-

evidence_quotes.quote: 'hidden message' of certain use of words or images

paragraphs.text: finally recognised the 'hidden message' of certain use of words or images, the harm has already occurred or the modus operandi has changed.

At the same time, identifying the ineffectiveness of AI as a control mechanism of digital service providers has been identified as not appropriate to use them from the EU itself. Chat control is currently being put forward as the way to intercept child abuse images from digital service providers such as Whatsapp. Questions can be asked about the energy and resources that will be used in a design, which is again noble but for which the application is not sufficiently developed. Targeted action should be taken to remove images of child abuse and those who make and disseminate them from the digital world. This is through the use of targeted measures. The technology is not sufficiently developed for this purpose.

evidence_quotes.quote: control mechanisms, especially those based entirely on automation

paragraphs.text: Therefore, according to the petitioner, this calls for an adjustment of the acceptability of control mechanisms, especially those based entirely on automation, whether or not enhanced by AI.

As stated above, scientific research is an extremely important key in the demand for fair practices in the digital environment. This study provides the basis for a wide range of advice, tips and information for users, compiled in Flanders under the heading 'media literacy'.

Flemish media policy aims to strengthen the resilience of media users by providing them with guidance and means to actively, creatively, critically and consciously use and understand digital technology and media to participate in our society.8 The petitioner also supports this policy objective as a Member's representative.

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns.

paragraphs.text: In doing so, the petitioner considers media literacy to be an indispensable skill for every citizen to participate in civic debate, both offline and online. Media literacy is divided into a number of sub-domains. Specifically for the digital environment, one of the issues discussed is platform literacy: it teaches users how digital platforms work by pointing them, among other things, to the existence of dark patterns. In this way, users can better assess risks, know how to avoid certain content and have more control over their online experience. 9 An important condition for this is that users are able to make choices independently and do not restrict their self-support and endorsement. 10

In the context of a performant media literacy policy, it is important to see both experts and parents who benefit from media literacy insights as mentors, not as auditors. They assist vulnerable people, both young people and adults, in their online experiences.
This approach can greatly contribute to addressing concerns about the correlation between mental well-being and online experience. This includes the development of a network of channels, whether
work/commissions/commissievergaderingen/1906383/verslag/1910283)
8 https://www.mediawijs.be/nl/over-ons

9 McStay, A. and Wood, D. 2024. Can platform literacy protect vulnerable young people against the risky affordances of social media platforms? LSE Media Science-Communications. Available at: https://eprints.lse.ac.uk/128350/3/Can_platform_literacy_protect_vulnerable_young_people_against_the_risky_affordances_of_social_media- platforms .pdf

10 Archer, H. 2021. Online media literacy: Across the world, demand for training is going unmet. Ipsos. 15 March. Available at: https://www.ipsos.com/en/online-media-literacy-across-world-demand-training-going-unmet

evidence_quotes.quote: harmful content can easily be reported
users are supported in identifying and addressing online risks.

paragraphs.text: or not separate from the digital service providers themselves, where harmful content can easily be reported and users are supported in identifying and addressing online risks.

evidence_quotes.quote: harmful design of digital services at source.

paragraphs.text: The petitioner considers the European Union to be a partner in providing effective regulation for a general framework for safe and fair online experience and services. She calls for a DFA aimed at ensuring fair online practices for all users, based on proportionate evidence-based measures that tackle harmful design of digital services at source. In doing so, targeted support should be provided to the most vulnerable groups, without depriving them of opportunities.

This requires coordinated research and access to the data of digital service providers, as the DSA already allows. Rather than the fear of the unknown resulting from rapid technological progress, or a sense of political urgency fuelled by that fear, a well-designed digital security policy and fair digital practices need knowledge that allows for the development of smart and balanced measures that offer better online perception to users, including minors.

evidence_quotes.quote: assessment of an appropriate user age.

paragraphs.text: With specific regard to the question of the relationship between the role of the European Union and the role of the Member States in the DFA file, the petitioner is in favour of a minimum harmonisation by the European Union and a high degree of discretion in the further implementation of the principles set out in the Regulation by the Member States, in particular as regards further enforcement and sanctioning of content offered by digital service providers and the relationship with regard to the assessment of an appropriate user age.

evidence_quotes.quote: minimum technical conditions (e.g. algorithms, standard user settings, formal requirements user conditions) with which digital service providers must comply.

paragraphs.text: For the petitioner, a future DFA can only comment on minimum technical conditions (e.g. algorithms, standard user settings, formal requirements user conditions) with which digital service providers must comply. Member States should express their views on the concrete content made available through digital service providers. This attitude is in line with Article 167 TFEU and the resulting AVMD, which provides guidance to Member States to regulate the content offered.

Minors may independently create an account for the petitioner from the age of 13, minors under the age of 13 need the implicit consent of their parents. This means that digital service providers must be subject to effective, privacy-conscious and proportionate age control.

evidence_quotes.quote: Age verification is only necessary for platforms with 18 + content (e.g. pornography, gambling).

paragraphs.text: This means that different methods of age verification are desirable for the petitioner depending on the content of the online platform and the risk to minors. It considers that it best meets the general principles of effectiveness, privacy awareness and proportionality. Age verification is only necessary for platforms with 18 + content (e.g. pornography, gambling).

In line with the approach that the European Commission will follow in enforcing the DSA, the petitioner also advocates that age checking should not lead to users being excluded from digital services, such as

social media, without access to one specific method within one category. The contract is clear: ensure a safer online environment and empower young people in their digital skills. Protection of minors is the starting point, but this should be done in a way that works, is not easy to circumvent and takes into account fundamental rights such as privacy, proportionality, freedom of expression and the right to information.
The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.
